patterns.name: Horizontal Scaling
patterns.description: Adding more machines to a resource pool to distribute load across multiple nodes. Each node handles a subset of requests, enabling near-linear throughput growth. The cornerstone of modern distributed system design.
principles: Stateless services scale more easily than stateful ones
Data partitioning strategy determines scaling ceiling
Each node should be independently deployable and replaceable
Shared-nothing architecture minimises inter-node coordination
Capacity planning should account for headroom and burst traffic
Service discovery must track the dynamic node pool
when_to_use: Single-node vertical limits have been reached or are approaching
Traffic patterns are unpredictable and require elastic capacity
High availability requires surviving individual node failures
Read-heavy workloads can be distributed across replicas
Cost-per-unit decreases with commodity hardware at scale
Geographic distribution is needed for latency reduction
avoid_when: Application state is tightly coupled and cannot be externalized
Strong consistency requirements make coordination overhead prohibitive
Workload is inherently sequential and cannot be parallelized
Operational complexity budget is already exhausted
signals: CPU or memory utilization consistently above 70% on single node
Request latency increases linearly with traffic volume
Deployment downtime required for capacity changes
Single point of failure in the serving path
Database connection pool exhaustion under peak load
Application uses stateless HTTP handlers or gRPC services

patterns.name: Vertical Scaling
patterns.description: Increasing the capacity of a single node by adding CPU, memory, storage, or network bandwidth. Simpler operationally than horizontal scaling but bounded by hardware limits. Often the first scaling strategy before distributing.
principles: Hardware limits impose a hard ceiling on vertical capacity
Single-node performance tuning should precede horizontal expansion
Larger instances often have non-linear cost curves
Vertical scaling preserves application simplicity
Downtime may be required for hardware upgrades in non-cloud environments
Monitoring must track resource saturation to anticipate limits
when_to_use: Application is monolithic and refactoring cost is high
Database workload benefits from larger memory for caching
Current hardware utilization is well below vendor maximum
Operational team lacks expertise in distributed systems
Latency-sensitive workloads benefit from faster single-thread performance
Quick capacity relief is needed before a longer-term horizontal migration
avoid_when: Hardware maximum has been reached or is economically impractical
High availability requires multi-node redundancy
Traffic growth rate will outpace vertical upgrade cadence
Cloud cost for large instances exceeds multiple smaller ones
signals: Single process consuming majority of available CPU cores
In-memory dataset approaching physical RAM limit
Disk I/O throughput saturated on current storage tier
Application architecture assumes single-process execution
Database query plans rely on large sort buffers or hash joins
Network interface throughput approaching NIC capacity
key_concepts: CPU core scaling and clock speed
Instance type selection in cloud providers

patterns.name: Autoscaling Policy
patterns.description: Automatically adjusting compute capacity based on observed metrics such as CPU utilization, request rate, or queue depth. Reduces cost during low traffic and ensures availability during spikes. Requires well-defined scaling triggers and cooldown periods.
principles: Scale-out thresholds should trigger before saturation occurs
Scale-in must be conservative to avoid flapping
Cooldown periods prevent oscillation between scale events
Multiple metrics provide more robust scaling decisions than single metrics
Predictive scaling complements reactive scaling for known patterns
Minimum instance count must cover baseline traffic and health checks
when_to_use: Traffic has predictable diurnal or weekly patterns
Burst traffic events occur that are difficult to predict exactly
Cost optimization requires matching capacity to demand
Application startup time is fast enough for reactive scaling
Cloud infrastructure supports programmatic instance management
SLA requires maintaining response time under variable load
avoid_when: Application cold start time exceeds acceptable latency window
Workload is constant and predictable with no variance
Licensing costs scale per-instance making elastic scaling expensive
Stateful services cannot redistribute work to new nodes quickly
signals: Cloud provider autoscaling group or equivalent is configured
Metrics pipeline exports CPU, memory, and custom application metrics
Traffic dashboards show regular peaks and valleys
Monthly infrastructure cost exceeds budget due to over-provisioning
Deployment manifests include resource requests and limits
Queue depth or request latency used as scaling triggers

patterns.name: Database Read Replicas
patterns.description: Replicating data from a primary database to one or more read-only replicas to distribute query load. Reduces contention on the primary for write operations. Introduces replication lag as a consistency tradeoff.
principles: Read replicas serve read-only queries to offload the primary
Replication lag must be monitored and bounded by SLA
Application routing logic must distinguish read and write paths
Replica count should match read-to-write ratio
Failover promotion of a replica requires careful coordination
Schema changes must propagate consistently across all replicas
when_to_use: Read-to-write ratio exceeds 10:1
Analytical queries compete with transactional queries on the primary
Geographic distribution of read traffic reduces latency
Reporting workloads can tolerate seconds of staleness
Primary database CPU is saturated by SELECT queries
Backup and export operations should not impact production reads
avoid_when: Application requires read-after-write consistency on every query
Write volume is the bottleneck rather than read volume
Replication lag tolerance is zero for the workload
Operational team cannot manage multi-node database topology
signals: Database CPU dominated by SELECT statement execution
Query latency spikes correlate with batch reporting jobs
Connection pool on primary is saturated by read connections
Application code already separates read and write data sources
Slow query log dominated by analytical aggregations
Monitoring shows replication lag metrics are available

patterns.name: Database Write Scaling
patterns.description: Distributing write operations across multiple database nodes through sharding, partitioning, or multi-primary replication. Eliminates the single-primary bottleneck for write-heavy workloads. Significantly increases architectural complexity.
principles: Shard key selection determines data distribution and query routing
Cross-shard transactions should be minimized or eliminated
Rebalancing shards must be possible without downtime
Write amplification from indexes must be accounted for
Conflict resolution strategy is required for multi-primary setups
Backup and restore complexity increases with shard count
when_to_use: Write throughput exceeds single-node disk I/O capacity
Data volume exceeds single-node storage limits
Write latency is bounded by disk sync on a single primary
Natural partition key exists in the data model (tenant ID, region)
Application can tolerate eventual consistency across partitions
Growth trajectory will exceed vertical scaling limits within planning horizon
avoid_when: Read replicas can absorb the load and writes are modest
Cross-partition queries are frequent and essential
Transaction boundaries span multiple logical partitions
Data model has no natural shard key
signals: Write-ahead log throughput approaching disk bandwidth limit
INSERT/UPDATE latency increases under concurrent write load
Table row counts exceeding billions with degrading write performance
Disk utilization on primary consistently above 80%
Application data model includes tenant_id or region_id columns
Batch ingestion jobs blocking transactional write paths

patterns.name: Connection Pooling
patterns.description: Maintaining a pool of reusable connections to databases or external services to amortize connection setup cost. Bounds the maximum number of concurrent connections to protect downstream resources. Essential for high-throughput applications.
principles: Pool size must balance concurrency needs against downstream limits
Idle connection timeout prevents resource leaks
Connection validation on borrow detects stale or broken connections
Pool exhaustion must produce clear errors rather than silent hangs
Separate pools for read and write paths enable independent tuning
Connection pool metrics are critical for capacity planning
when_to_use: Database connection setup time is significant (TCP + TLS + auth)
Application handles many concurrent requests sharing a database
Downstream service has a hard connection limit
Serverless or short-lived processes create excessive connection churn
Connection-per-request model causes resource exhaustion
Multiple microservices share a database cluster
avoid_when: Application is single-threaded with one connection at a time
Connection setup cost is negligible (in-process database)
Long-running connections with server-side state cannot be shared
Connection affinity is required for session-level settings
signals: Database max_connections limit frequently approached or hit
Connection setup time visible in request latency percentiles
TIME_WAIT socket states accumulating on application hosts
Database logs show rapid connect/disconnect cycling
Application startup includes connection pool configuration parameters
Monitoring shows connection count as a tracked metric
key_concepts: Metrics: wait time, active count, idle count

patterns.name: Thread Pool
patterns.description: Managing a fixed set of worker threads that process tasks from a shared queue, bounding concurrency and reducing thread creation overhead. Controls resource consumption and prevents thread explosion under load. Fundamental to server-side request processing.
principles: Pool size should match the workload profile (CPU-bound vs I/O-bound)
Task queue depth must be bounded to apply backpressure
Thread pool exhaustion is a primary cause of cascading failure
Separate pools for different workload types prevent mutual interference
Thread-local state must be managed carefully to avoid leaks
Monitoring active, idle, and queued counts reveals saturation
when_to_use: Server processes concurrent requests with blocking I/O
CPU-bound tasks need bounded parallelism matching core count
Thread creation cost is high relative to task duration
Workload mix includes both fast and slow operations
Resource isolation between task types is needed
Graceful shutdown requires draining in-flight work
avoid_when: Async/non-blocking I/O framework handles concurrency natively
Tasks are extremely short-lived and queue overhead dominates
Single-threaded event loop model is sufficient for the workload
Language runtime manages green threads or coroutines transparently
signals: Thread dump shows many threads in WAITING or BLOCKED state
Request latency increases sharply at specific concurrency thresholds
OS-level thread count per process growing without bound
Application configuration includes thread pool size parameters
CPU utilization is low despite high request latency (thread starvation)
Task rejection errors appear in application logs

patterns.name: Asynchronous Processing
patterns.description: Decoupling request acceptance from request processing by using message queues, event streams, or task schedulers. Enables the system to absorb traffic spikes by buffering work. Converts synchronous latency into eventual completion.
principles: Decouple producers from consumers via durable message brokers
Idempotent consumers enable safe retry without duplication
Message ordering guarantees must match business requirements
Dead letter queues capture permanently failed messages for analysis
Consumer lag monitoring detects processing bottlenecks
Backpressure signals should propagate from consumers to producers
when_to_use: Request processing time exceeds acceptable synchronous response latency
Downstream services have lower throughput than upstream producers
Work can be batched for more efficient processing
Failure of a processing step should not fail the user request
Traffic spikes are temporary and work can be drained over time
Multiple consumers need to process the same event independently
avoid_when: User expects synchronous confirmation of completed processing
End-to-end latency requirement is sub-millisecond
Message ordering is critical and difficult to guarantee
Operational complexity of message brokers exceeds team capacity
Debugging distributed async flows is beyond team skill level
signals: API endpoints performing long-running operations inline
Database transactions holding locks for extended durations
Timeout errors on downstream service calls during peak traffic
User-facing latency dominated by background processing steps
Application logs show retry storms against slow dependencies
Message queue or event stream infrastructure already deployed

patterns.name: Worker Pool
patterns.description: A set of worker processes or threads that consume tasks from a shared queue, enabling parallel processing of independent work units. Scales horizontally by adding more workers. Common in background job processing and data pipeline architectures.
principles: Workers should be stateless and independently restartable
Task visibility timeout prevents duplicate processing on worker failure
Concurrency level should match available resources and downstream capacity
Work distribution should be fair to prevent hot-spot workers
Graceful shutdown must complete in-flight tasks before stopping
Worker health monitoring enables automatic replacement of failed workers
when_to_use: Background job processing requires parallel execution
Data pipeline stages process independent records concurrently
Email, notification, or webhook delivery at scale
Image, video, or document processing workloads
ETL jobs that can be parallelized by partition key
Scheduled batch processing with variable completion times
avoid_when: Tasks have strict ordering dependencies
Task processing requires shared mutable state
Single task processing time is so fast that coordination overhead dominates
Total task volume does not justify pool management complexity
signals: Background job queue depth growing faster than drain rate
Sequential processing of independent work items
Job processing framework configured with worker count parameters
Separate deployment for worker processes vs web servers
Monitoring dashboards tracking queue depth and processing rate
Auto-scaling rules based on queue depth metrics
key_concepts: Dead letter handling for failed tasks

patterns.name: Elastic Scaling
patterns.description: Dynamically provisioning and deprovisioning resources in response to real-time demand, achieving cost efficiency without sacrificing performance. Goes beyond autoscaling by encompassing infrastructure-level elasticity including serverless and spot instances. Optimizes cost-to-capacity ratio continuously.
principles: Resources should scale both up and down automatically
Scaling decisions should incorporate cost signals alongside performance metrics
Spot and preemptible instances reduce cost for fault-tolerant workloads
Serverless functions provide per-invocation elasticity for bursty traffic
Infrastructure as code enables reproducible scaling configurations
Multi-cloud or multi-region elasticity increases resilience
when_to_use: Workload has extreme variance between peak and off-peak periods
Cost optimization is a primary concern alongside availability
Event-driven workloads with unpredictable invocation frequency
Development and staging environments used only during business hours
Batch processing jobs that can leverage spot instance pricing
Seasonal business patterns with known peak periods
avoid_when: Steady-state workload with minimal variance
Reserved instance pricing already optimizes cost for baseline
Application cannot tolerate instance preemption or cold starts
Regulatory requirements mandate dedicated hardware
signals: Monthly cloud bill shows significant spend on idle resources
Traffic graphs show distinct peak and trough patterns
Serverless function invocations vary by orders of magnitude
Spot instance interruption rate is acceptable for the workload
Infrastructure provisioning is automated via Terraform or Pulumi
Cost allocation tags track per-team or per-feature spending

patterns.name: Data Partitioning
patterns.description: Dividing a dataset across multiple storage nodes based on a partition key so that each node manages a subset of the data. Enables both storage and throughput to scale linearly with partition count. The partition strategy determines query routing, data locality, and rebalancing complexity.
principles: Partition key selection determines data distribution uniformity
Hot partitions degrade the entire system and must be detected early
Range-based partitioning supports range queries but risks skew
Hash-based partitioning distributes evenly but loses range query locality
Partition count should allow for future growth without repartitioning
Cross-partition operations should be avoided or explicitly bounded
when_to_use: Dataset exceeds single-node storage or throughput capacity
Natural partition key exists in the data model (tenant_id, date, region)
Query patterns align with partition boundaries for data locality
Write throughput must scale beyond single-node limits
Regulatory requirements mandate data residency by partition
Archival and lifecycle policies differ by partition (e.g., time-based)
avoid_when: Dataset fits comfortably on a single node with headroom
Queries frequently span all partitions negating the benefit
No natural partition key exists and synthetic keys add complexity
Operational team lacks tooling for partition management and rebalancing
signals: Table or collection configured with partition key in database schema
Shard ID or partition number in data routing logic
Consistent hashing ring implementation in data access layer
Hot partition alerts in database monitoring dashboards
Partition rebalancing operations in operational runbooks
Query router or coordinator layer in data access architecture

patterns.name: Cache-Aside (Lazy Loading)
patterns.description: Application code explicitly manages the cache by checking it before querying the data store, and populating it on cache miss. The cache contains only data that has been requested, minimizing memory usage. Most commonly used caching strategy in web applications.
principles: Application is responsible for cache read and write logic
Cache is populated lazily on first access (demand-driven)
Cache miss results in a data store query followed by cache population
TTL-based expiration provides eventual consistency with the data store
Cache stampede protection prevents thundering herd on popular keys
Serialization format must be consistent between write and read paths
when_to_use: Read-heavy workload where most reads hit the same data repeatedly
Data can tolerate staleness within a defined TTL window
Application needs fine-grained control over what is cached
Cache memory is limited and should only hold hot data
Different data types require different caching strategies
Integration with existing data stores without modifying them
avoid_when: Data changes frequently and stale reads are unacceptable
Write-heavy workload would cause constant cache invalidation
Application cannot tolerate the latency of cache misses on cold start
Cache management code duplicated across many services
signals: Database query logs show repeated identical queries
Application code contains get-from-cache-or-fetch patterns
Redis or Memcached client library in the dependency tree
Cache hit rate metrics tracked in monitoring dashboards
TTL configuration per entity type in application config
Data access layer wraps queries with cache lookup logic
key_concepts: Cache stampede and thundering herd prevention

patterns.name: Read-Through Cache
patterns.description: The cache itself is responsible for loading data from the backing store on a miss, transparently to the application. Simplifies application code by encapsulating cache population logic within the cache layer. Often implemented by caching libraries or frameworks.
principles: Cache layer encapsulates data loading logic
Application interacts only with the cache, never directly with the store on reads
Cache loader function must be idempotent and thread-safe
Synchronous loading blocks the requesting thread until data is available
Refresh-ahead can proactively reload frequently accessed entries before expiry
Error handling in the loader must not poison the cache with error states
when_to_use: Multiple application components access the same cached data
Cache population logic should be centralized and consistent
Framework or library supports read-through semantics natively
Data loading involves complex transformations before caching
Reducing code duplication of cache-aside patterns across services
Cache warming is handled by the cache infrastructure
avoid_when: Application needs direct control over when and how data is loaded
Different consumers need different representations of the same data
Cache framework does not support custom loader functions
Debugging requires visibility into cache vs store access paths
signals: Cache configuration includes a loader or origin function reference
Application code calls cache.get() without fallback to data store
Guava LoadingCache, Caffeine, or similar library in use
Cache metrics show automatic reload events
Single cache interface serves multiple consumers transparently
No explicit cache-miss handling in application business logic
key_concepts: Coalescing concurrent loads for same key

patterns.name: Write-Through Cache
patterns.description: Data is written to both the cache and the backing store synchronously in a single operation. Ensures the cache always contains the latest written data, providing strong consistency at the cost of write latency. Pairs naturally with read-through caching.
principles: Write completes only after both cache and store are updated
Cache and store consistency is guaranteed at write time
Write latency increases due to dual-write synchronous path
Atomicity of the dual write must be ensured to prevent inconsistency
Write-through populates cache proactively, eliminating read misses for written data
Suitable for workloads where written data is immediately read back
when_to_use: Read-after-write consistency is required
Written data is frequently read shortly after writing
Application cannot tolerate stale reads of recently written data
Cache and data store are co-located with low inter-latency
Data integrity is prioritized over write throughput
Audit requirements demand consistent cache and store state
avoid_when: Write throughput is the primary bottleneck
Written data is rarely read back (cache pollution)
Network latency between cache and store is high
Write-heavy workload would saturate the cache with infrequently accessed data
signals: Application writes to cache and database in the same transaction
Cache entries updated inline with database INSERT/UPDATE operations
Zero tolerance for stale cache reads of recently modified data
Cache hit rate for recently written data is critical for UX
ORM or data access layer has write-through hooks configured
Write path latency budget includes cache update overhead

patterns.name: Write-Behind (Write-Back) Cache
patterns.description: Data is written to the cache immediately and asynchronously flushed to the backing store. Provides low write latency by decoupling the store update from the request path. Introduces risk of data loss if the cache fails before flushing.
principles: Cache absorbs writes immediately, store is updated asynchronously
Write coalescing reduces store write volume for frequently updated keys
Flush interval and batch size are tunable consistency-performance knobs
Data loss window equals the unflushed write buffer
Cache must be durable or replicated to minimize loss risk
Ordering of writes to the store must be preserved for correctness
when_to_use: Write latency must be minimized for user-facing operations
High write volume would overwhelm the backing store synchronously
Writes to the same key are frequent and can be coalesced
Temporary data loss of a few seconds is acceptable
Backing store has rate limits or throttling that buffer absorbs
Write patterns are bursty and benefit from smoothing
avoid_when: Data loss of any write is unacceptable (financial transactions)
Regulatory requirements mandate synchronous persistence
Cache infrastructure lacks durability or replication
Debugging write ordering issues is operationally costly
signals: Application write latency dominated by database persist time
High-frequency counter or metric updates to persistent store
Write bursts cause database connection pool exhaustion
Cache configured with async writeback or dirty flag tracking
Monitoring shows write coalescing ratio as a tracked metric
Application can reconstruct state from event log if cache is lost

patterns.name: Distributed Cache
patterns.description: A caching layer spread across multiple nodes, providing shared cache access for all application instances. Eliminates per-instance cache duplication and inconsistency. Adds network latency compared to local caches but offers horizontal scalability.
principles: Consistent hashing distributes keys evenly across cache nodes
Cache cluster must tolerate node failures without full cache loss
Network round-trip adds latency compared to in-process caching
Serialization overhead must be minimized for large or complex objects
Memory capacity scales horizontally by adding cache nodes
Cache topology changes should not cause mass key redistribution
when_to_use: Multiple application instances need a shared consistent cache
Local cache duplication wastes memory across instances
Cache dataset exceeds single-node memory capacity
Session or state data must be accessible from any application instance
Cache invalidation must be immediate across all consumers
Horizontal scaling of cache capacity is required
avoid_when: Single application instance serves all traffic
Network latency for cache access exceeds latency budget
Data fits comfortably in local application memory
Operational overhead of a cache cluster is not justified
signals: Redis Cluster, Memcached, or Hazelcast in infrastructure
Multiple application pods accessing shared cache endpoints
Cache key collisions or inconsistencies across application instances
Local caches showing different values for the same key across pods
Cache infrastructure has dedicated monitoring and alerting
Cache connection strings in application configuration

patterns.name: CDN Caching
patterns.description: Caching content at geographically distributed edge servers to serve users from the nearest point of presence. Reduces origin server load and improves latency for static and semi-dynamic content. Controlled via HTTP cache headers and CDN-specific configuration.
principles: Cache-Control headers govern CDN caching behavior
Edge locations serve content without origin round-trips on cache hit
Cache key must include all dimensions that affect response (vary headers)
Purge mechanisms must exist for emergency content updates
Origin shield reduces origin load by adding a mid-tier cache
Edge compute enables dynamic content personalization at the CDN layer
when_to_use: Static assets (images, CSS, JS) served to geographically distributed users
API responses that are cacheable with known TTL
Media streaming content delivery at scale
Reducing origin server bandwidth and compute costs
Protecting origin from traffic spikes and DDoS
Improving Time to First Byte for global audiences
avoid_when: Content is highly personalized per user with no shared segments
Real-time data freshness is required on every request
Cache purge latency exceeds acceptable staleness window
CDN cost per GB exceeds origin serving cost for the traffic volume
signals: CloudFront, Fastly, Cloudflare, or Akamai in infrastructure
Cache-Control and ETag headers in HTTP responses
Origin server bandwidth reduction after CDN deployment
Geographic latency variation in user experience metrics
Static asset URLs include content hash or version for cache busting
CDN hit ratio tracked as a key performance metric
key_concepts: Point of presence and edge location selection
Edge compute and dynamic edge logic

patterns.name: Application-Level Cache
patterns.description: In-process caching within the application runtime, storing frequently accessed data in local memory. Provides the lowest possible access latency with zero network overhead. Limited by process memory and not shared across instances.
principles: Local cache access is orders of magnitude faster than network cache
Memory bounds must be enforced to prevent OOM conditions
Eviction policies (LRU, LFU, FIFO) manage memory constraints
Cache entries should have TTL to prevent serving stale data indefinitely
Each application instance maintains its own independent cache
Cache warming on startup reduces cold-start latency impact
when_to_use: Lookup data (config, feature flags) accessed on every request
Computed results that are expensive to recalculate
Reference data that changes infrequently (country codes, enums)
Network latency to distributed cache exceeds budget for hot-path data
Single-instance applications where cache consistency is trivial
Reducing serialization overhead for complex object graphs
avoid_when: Multiple instances must see the same cache state
Cached data changes frequently requiring instant invalidation across instances
Dataset exceeds available process memory
Cache consistency across instances is a business requirement
signals: HashMap, ConcurrentHashMap, or dict used as lookup cache in code
Caffeine, Guava Cache, or lru_cache decorators in source
Repeated computation of the same result within request processing
Configuration or reference data loaded on startup and held in memory
Memory profiling shows large cached object graphs
Cache hit/miss counters in application metrics
key_concepts: LRU, LFU, and FIFO eviction policies

patterns.name: Query Result Cache
patterns.description: Caching the results of database queries keyed by the query text and parameters. Eliminates redundant database round-trips for identical queries within the TTL window. Effective for read-heavy workloads with predictable query patterns.
principles: Cache key must include query text, parameters, and any relevant context
Invalidation must occur when underlying data changes
Complex queries with large result sets have high serialization cost
Query plan caching is distinct from result caching
Parameterized queries improve cache hit rate over dynamic SQL
Cache should be bypassed for queries that must reflect real-time state
when_to_use: Dashboard or reporting queries executed repeatedly with identical parameters
API endpoints serving the same filtered/sorted data to multiple users
Database CPU is saturated by repeated identical query execution
Query response time exceeds acceptable latency and results are cacheable
Aggregate queries (COUNT, SUM, AVG) over slowly changing data
Full-text search results for popular search terms
avoid_when: Query results must reflect real-time data changes
Each query has unique parameters reducing cache hit probability
Result set is very large and serialization cost exceeds query cost
Write-heavy tables invalidate cached results too frequently
signals: Slow query log shows identical queries repeated frequently
Database CPU dominated by identical SELECT execution plans
Application-level query cache configuration (e.g., Hibernate L2 cache)
Redis keys structured as query hash values
API response caching middleware in the request pipeline
Query latency metrics show bimodal distribution (cache hit vs miss)
key_concepts: Query fingerprinting and cache key generation

patterns.name: Object Cache
patterns.description: Caching fully constructed domain objects or aggregates to avoid repeated assembly from multiple data sources. Particularly valuable when object construction involves joins, computations, or external service calls. Reduces latency for complex object hydration paths.
principles: Cache the fully assembled object, not individual data fragments
Invalidation must account for all data sources contributing to the object
Versioning cached objects handles schema evolution gracefully
Object identity (ID + version) should be part of the cache key
Deep object graphs require careful serialization strategies
Partial object caching can optimize memory at the cost of complexity
when_to_use: Object assembly requires multiple database queries or service calls
Domain aggregates are read far more often than modified
API responses serialize complex object graphs repeatedly
Product catalog, user profiles, or configuration objects accessed frequently
Object construction involves expensive computation or transformation
Microservice responses can cache assembled view models
avoid_when: Objects are modified as frequently as they are read
Object state includes user-specific data reducing shareability
Serialization cost of complex objects exceeds reconstruction cost
Cache invalidation for multi-source objects is too complex to maintain
signals: Multiple database queries executed to construct a single API response
Object assembly code performs joins across service boundaries
Profiling shows object construction as a significant latency contributor
Same aggregate root loaded repeatedly across different request handlers
Cache keys structured as entity_type:entity_id patterns
Domain model includes rich aggregate boundaries
key_concepts: Lazy loading vs eager caching tradeoffs

patterns.name: Edge Caching
patterns.description: Caching data and computation at network edge locations closest to end users, beyond traditional CDN static asset caching. Enables dynamic content generation, API response caching, and personalization at the edge. Leverages edge compute platforms like Cloudflare Workers or Lambda@Edge.
principles: Edge cache reduces round-trip time to origin by serving locally
Cache segmentation by geography, device, or user cohort improves hit rate
Edge compute enables dynamic cache key computation and response assembly
Consistency is eventually achieved via background revalidation
Edge cache capacity is limited; eviction policies must be tuned
Origin failover at the edge improves availability during origin outages
when_to_use: Latency-sensitive applications serving global user base
API responses that can be cached with geographic or cohort segmentation
A/B testing where variant assignment can be computed at the edge
Bot detection and security filtering before reaching origin
GraphQL or REST API responses with shared segments across users
Real-time personalization with edge-side includes
avoid_when: All users require unique, non-cacheable responses
Edge compute cost exceeds latency benefit
Data sovereignty requires responses generated in specific regions only
Application logic complexity exceeds edge compute runtime limits
signals: Cloudflare Workers, Lambda@Edge, or Fastly Compute in infrastructure
Geographic latency variation exceeding 200ms across regions
Surrogate-Key or Cache-Tag headers for granular edge invalidation
Edge-side includes (ESI) in response templates
Edge function logs showing request routing decisions
Cache segmentation by Accept-Language or geo headers

patterns.name: Cache Invalidation
patterns.description: Strategies for removing or updating stale cache entries when the underlying data changes. One of the hardest problems in computer science due to the tradeoff between consistency, performance, and complexity. Requires careful design to avoid serving stale data or over-invalidating.
principles: TTL-based expiration provides eventual consistency with minimal complexity
Event-driven invalidation provides near-real-time consistency
Over-invalidation wastes cache capacity; under-invalidation serves stale data
Cache tags enable invalidation of related entries in a single operation
Versioned cache keys avoid invalidation entirely by writing new keys
Invalidation scope must match the data dependency graph
when_to_use: Cached data has known mutation events that can trigger invalidation
Business requirements specify maximum staleness tolerance
Data changes are infrequent relative to reads
Cache tag infrastructure supports group invalidation
Database change data capture can feed invalidation events
Multiple cache layers (L1/L2) require coordinated invalidation
avoid_when: Data changes so frequently that invalidation rate exceeds cache utility
Invalidation dependency graph is too complex to model correctly
TTL-based eventual consistency is sufficient and simpler
Invalidation infrastructure adds more complexity than direct data store access
signals: Stale data complaints from users or downstream consumers
Cache delete or purge calls in write path code
Database triggers or CDC pipeline feeding invalidation events
Cache tag or key pattern-based bulk deletion in admin tools
Monitoring shows invalidation rate as a tracked metric
Incident reports citing stale cache as root cause
key_concepts: Change data capture for invalidation triggers

patterns.name: Raft Consensus
patterns.description: A consensus algorithm designed for understandability, achieving distributed agreement through leader election and log replication. Ensures a cluster of nodes agrees on a sequence of state transitions even in the presence of failures. Used in etcd, Consul, and CockroachDB.
principles: Single leader handles all client writes and replicates to followers
Leader election uses randomized timeouts to break ties
Log entries are committed when a majority of nodes acknowledge them
Term numbers monotonically increase and prevent stale leader operations
Followers that fall behind can catch up via log replication or snapshots
Safety is never compromised; availability is sacrificed during leader election
when_to_use: Configuration management requiring strong consistency (etcd, Consul)
Metadata services where correctness is more important than throughput
Leader election for distributed coordinators
Distributed lock services requiring mutual exclusion guarantees
Replicated state machines for critical system state
Small cluster sizes (3, 5, 7 nodes) where Raft overhead is acceptable
avoid_when: High write throughput is required and consensus is the bottleneck
Cluster size exceeds practical Raft limits (more than ~7 voting members)
Eventual consistency is sufficient for the use case
Network partitions are frequent and availability is prioritized over consistency
signals: etcd, Consul, or CockroachDB in the infrastructure stack
Raft log directory and snapshot files on disk
Cluster membership configuration with odd node counts
Leader election timeout and heartbeat interval in configuration
Monitoring tracks term number, commit index, and replication lag
Application uses distributed lock or leader election APIs

patterns.name: Paxos
patterns.description: The foundational consensus algorithm proving that distributed agreement is achievable in asynchronous systems with crash failures. Guarantees safety (agreement and validity) without requiring timing assumptions. Practically difficult to implement correctly, leading to variants like Multi-Paxos and Fast Paxos.
principles: Proposers, acceptors, and learners are distinct logical roles
A value is chosen when accepted by a majority of acceptors
Prepare-promise-accept-learn phases ensure agreement
Higher proposal numbers override lower ones, preventing conflicts
No timing assumptions needed for safety (only for liveness)
Multi-Paxos optimizes for repeated consensus with a stable leader
when_to_use: Building consensus-based systems where correctness is paramount
Academic or research implementations requiring proven correctness
Systems where Raft's leader-based model is too restrictive
Multi-decree consensus with stable leadership (Multi-Paxos)
Distributed database commit protocols requiring provable safety
Understanding the theoretical foundation of distributed consensus
avoid_when: Raft provides sufficient functionality with better understandability
Team lacks deep distributed systems expertise for correct implementation
Off-the-shelf consensus systems (etcd, ZooKeeper) can be used instead
Eventual consistency is acceptable for the use case
signals: Research papers or design docs referencing Paxos by name
Custom consensus implementation in the codebase
Proposal number tracking and ballot management logic
Acceptor state persistence for crash recovery
Google Chubby or Spanner-inspired system architecture
Multi-phase commit protocol with prepare and accept phases

patterns.name: Two-Phase Commit (2PC)
patterns.description: A distributed transaction protocol coordinating atomic commit or abort across multiple participants. The coordinator sends prepare requests, collects votes, then broadcasts commit or abort. Provides atomicity but is blocking and vulnerable to coordinator failure.
principles: Coordinator drives the prepare and commit/abort phases
All participants must vote yes for the transaction to commit
Any participant voting no causes the entire transaction to abort
Participants hold locks between prepare and commit, blocking other transactions
Coordinator failure after prepare creates an in-doubt state
Write-ahead logging on coordinator and participants enables recovery
when_to_use: Distributed transactions across heterogeneous databases
XA transactions in enterprise middleware (JTA, MSDTC)
Cross-shard atomic operations requiring all-or-nothing semantics
Legacy system integration where saga pattern refactoring is infeasible
Financial transactions requiring strict atomicity across systems
Database federation requiring cross-database consistency
avoid_when: High throughput is required and lock contention is unacceptable
Coordinator single point of failure risk is too high
Participants span wide-area networks with high latency
Saga pattern or eventual consistency is acceptable
Microservice architecture favors autonomy over distributed transactions
signals: XA transaction manager in middleware configuration
Distributed transaction coordinator process in deployment
Prepare and commit log entries in database transaction logs
In-doubt transaction recovery procedures in operations playbooks
Cross-database JOIN or UPDATE operations in application code
Lock timeout errors under concurrent distributed transaction load
key_concepts: Presumed abort and presumed commit optimizations

patterns.name: Three-Phase Commit (3PC)
patterns.description: An extension of two-phase commit that adds a pre-commit phase to reduce blocking in the event of coordinator failure. Participants in the pre-commit state can independently decide to commit after a timeout. Trades additional message complexity for reduced blocking window.
principles: Pre-commit phase allows participants to know the coordinator's decision
Timeout-based recovery enables progress without coordinator
Three round-trips increase latency compared to 2PC
Does not solve the fundamental problem under network partitions
Participants can make autonomous decisions after coordinator timeout
Practical implementations are rare due to complexity and network partition issues
when_to_use: Coordinator failure blocking is unacceptable in the deployment environment
Network is reliable (same datacenter, low partition probability)
Availability of the transaction protocol is critical
Academic or research systems exploring non-blocking commit
Systems where 2PC blocking has caused production incidents
Environments where bounded commit latency is required
avoid_when: Network partitions are common (wide-area deployments)
Additional message round-trip latency is unacceptable
Saga pattern or eventual consistency provides sufficient guarantees
Implementation complexity exceeds team capacity
2PC blocking has not been a practical problem in production
signals: Pre-commit phase in transaction protocol implementation
Timeout-based autonomous commit decision logic
Three distinct phase handlers in transaction coordinator code
Research or design documents discussing non-blocking commit
Transaction protocol with can-commit, pre-commit, and do-commit states
Coordinator failure recovery procedures that reference 3PC

patterns.name: Vector Clocks
patterns.description: A mechanism for tracking causality in distributed systems by maintaining a vector of logical timestamps, one per node. Enables detection of concurrent updates and causal ordering without physical clock synchronization. Used in eventually consistent datastores like Dynamo-style systems.
principles: Each node increments its own position in the vector on local events
Vectors are merged on message receipt by taking component-wise maximum
Two events are causally ordered if one vector dominates the other
Concurrent events have incomparable vectors requiring conflict resolution
Vector size grows with the number of participating nodes
Version vectors (a variant) track data item versions for conflict detection
when_to_use: Detecting causality relationships between distributed events
Conflict detection in multi-master replication systems
Eventually consistent datastores requiring version tracking
Distributed debugging and event ordering analysis
Optimistic concurrency control across distributed nodes
Implementing last-writer-wins or merge-based conflict resolution
avoid_when: Strong consistency eliminates concurrent writes by design
Number of nodes is very large making vector size impractical
Simpler Lamport timestamps provide sufficient ordering
Physical clock-based ordering (TrueTime) is available and sufficient
signals: Version vector or vector clock data structure in codebase
Conflict resolution logic comparing event timestamps
Dynamo-style or Riak-style database in infrastructure
Concurrent update detection and merge functions in data layer
Per-node sequence number tracking in replication protocol
Causal consistency model documented in system design

patterns.name: Conflict-Free Replicated Data Type (CRDT)
patterns.description: Data structures that can be replicated across nodes and merged automatically without coordination, guaranteeing eventual convergence. Designed so that all concurrent operations commute, eliminating the need for consensus on write ordering. Used in collaborative editing, distributed counters, and edge computing.
principles: Merge function must be commutative, associative, and idempotent
State-based CRDTs ship full state; operation-based CRDTs ship operations
All replicas converge to the same state given the same set of updates
No coordination required between replicas for writes
Monotonic join semilattice property ensures convergence
Garbage collection of tombstones and metadata requires coordination
when_to_use: Collaborative editing requiring concurrent updates without locks
Distributed counters that must be incremented across multiple nodes
Shopping carts, voting systems, or presence indicators
Edge computing where nodes operate independently and sync periodically
Offline-first applications requiring conflict-free sync
Systems where availability is prioritized over immediate consistency
avoid_when: Strong consistency and immediate convergence is required
Data model does not map naturally to known CRDT types
Metadata overhead of CRDT state tracking is too large
Operations are inherently non-commutative (e.g., ordered sequences with constraints)
signals: Automerge, Yjs, or Riak data types library in dependencies
Merge functions in data layer that combine concurrent states
Counter types with increment-only or positive-negative tracking
Set types with add/remove operations and tombstone tracking
Distributed application operating in offline or partition-tolerant mode
Collaborative features (shared documents, whiteboards) in product

patterns.name: Gossip Protocol
patterns.description: A decentralized communication protocol where nodes periodically exchange state with randomly selected peers, propagating information epidemically. Provides eventual consistency without centralized coordination. Highly resilient to node failures and network partitions.
principles: Each node periodically selects random peers to exchange state
Information spreads exponentially through the cluster
Convergence time is O(log N) for N nodes under ideal conditions
No central coordinator or single point of failure
Tolerates high node churn and network partitions gracefully
Anti-entropy mechanisms reconcile divergent state between nodes
when_to_use: Cluster membership and failure detection in large systems
Disseminating configuration updates across a distributed cluster
Aggregate computation (global counts, averages) without centralization
Systems with high node count where centralized broadcast is impractical
Peer-to-peer networks requiring decentralized state propagation
Eventual consistency of metadata across datacenter nodes
avoid_when: Immediate consistency is required for propagated state
Cluster size is small enough for direct broadcast to all nodes
Deterministic propagation time is a hard requirement
Network bandwidth for gossip messages is constrained
signals: Serf, memberlist, or SWIM protocol library in dependencies
Random peer selection logic in cluster communication code
Gossip interval and fanout configuration parameters
Cluster membership view that converges rather than being centrally managed
Anti-entropy or state reconciliation background processes
Failure detector based on missed gossip heartbeats
key_concepts: Push, pull, and push-pull gossip variants

patterns.name: Leader Election
patterns.description: A protocol for selecting a single node in a distributed system to serve as the coordinator for a specific task or partition. Ensures exactly one leader is active at a time to prevent split-brain scenarios. Critical for systems that require centralized decision-making within a distributed architecture.
principles: Exactly one leader must be active at any given time (mutual exclusion)
Fencing tokens prevent stale leaders from making decisions after replacement
Leader lease renewal must occur before expiry to prevent interregnum
Split-brain detection and resolution is essential for correctness
Leader failure must be detected and a new election triggered promptly
Election protocol must tolerate network partitions safely
when_to_use: Coordinating writes to a shared resource from multiple nodes
Scheduling and task assignment in a distributed worker cluster
Master-slave replication requiring a designated primary
Singleton services that must run on exactly one node
Partition leadership assignment in stream processing systems
Distributed lock coordination requiring a central arbiter
avoid_when: Leaderless architecture can handle the coordination requirement
CRDTs or gossip protocol can replace centralized coordination
Single point of failure from leader dependency is unacceptable without rapid failover
Leader election overhead exceeds the coordination benefit
signals: ZooKeeper ephemeral nodes or Consul sessions for leader tracking
Kubernetes lease objects for controller leader election
Fencing token or generation number in write operations
Leader health check and failover procedures in runbooks
Application startup includes leader election registration logic
Split-brain incident reports in operations history
key_concepts: Fencing tokens for stale leader prevention

patterns.name: Quorum
patterns.description: Requiring a minimum number of nodes to agree on a read or write operation to ensure consistency in a replicated system. Read and write quorums that overlap guarantee that reads observe the most recent write. The fundamental building block for tunable consistency in distributed datastores.
principles: Write quorum W plus read quorum R must exceed total replicas N (W + R > N)
Larger W increases write latency but reduces read quorum requirement
Quorum intersection ensures at least one node has the latest write
Sloppy quorum trades consistency for availability during partitions
Hinted handoff enables writes during temporary node unavailability
Quorum configuration is a tunable consistency-availability knob
when_to_use: Dynamo-style databases requiring tunable consistency (Cassandra, Riak)
Replicated systems where strict majority consensus is too expensive
Applications needing flexible consistency levels per operation
Read-heavy workloads that can use R=1 with W=N for fast reads
Write-heavy workloads that can use W=1 with R=N for fast writes
Multi-datacenter replication with local quorum semantics
avoid_when: Linearizable consistency is required (use Raft or Paxos instead)
Replica count is very small (2 nodes) making quorum fragile
Application cannot handle the complexity of tunable consistency
Network partitions are frequent and sloppy quorum semantics are confusing
signals: Consistency level configuration (ONE, QUORUM, ALL) in database queries
Replication factor and quorum size parameters in database config
Read repair or anti-entropy processes in database maintenance
Hinted handoff directories on database nodes
Cassandra, Riak, or DynamoDB in the infrastructure stack
Consistency level tuning based on operation criticality
key_concepts: Read and write quorum overlap (W + R > N)

patterns.name: CAP Theorem Tradeoffs
patterns.description: The practical application of the CAP theorem, which states that a distributed system can provide at most two of three guarantees: consistency, availability, and partition tolerance. Since network partitions are inevitable, the real choice is between consistency and availability during partitions. Guides architecture decisions for distributed data systems.
principles: Network partitions are inevitable; the choice is between C and A during partitions
CP systems sacrifice availability to maintain consistency during partitions
AP systems sacrifice consistency to maintain availability during partitions
CAP is a spectrum, not a binary choice, in practice
PACELC extends CAP to address latency-consistency tradeoffs during normal operation
Different parts of a system can make different CAP tradeoffs
when_to_use: Designing a new distributed data system and choosing consistency model
Evaluating database technology for a distributed deployment
Analyzing failure modes and their impact on data consistency
Documenting system behavior during network partition scenarios
Making per-service or per-operation consistency decisions
Communicating tradeoffs to stakeholders in system design reviews
avoid_when: System is not distributed (single node, no replication)
Using CAP as the sole framework without considering PACELC
Oversimplifying the nuances of consistency models
Network partitions are genuinely impossible (single rack, no WAN)
signals: Architecture documents discussing consistency vs availability tradeoffs
Database selection evaluations comparing CP vs AP systems
Incident reports describing behavior during network partition events
Consistency level configuration varying by operation type
System design documents referencing CAP or PACELC explicitly
Multi-region deployment with cross-region replication decisions
key_concepts: Linearizability vs sequential vs causal consistency

patterns.name: Eventual Consistency
patterns.description: A consistency model guaranteeing that, in the absence of new updates, all replicas will eventually converge to the same value. Provides high availability and low latency at the cost of temporary inconsistency windows. The foundation of most large-scale distributed systems.
principles: All replicas converge to the same value given sufficient time without updates
Inconsistency window must be bounded and monitored
Conflict resolution strategy handles concurrent updates (LWW, merge, manual)
Read-your-writes consistency can be layered on top for better UX
Anti-entropy processes accelerate convergence between replicas
Application logic must tolerate reading stale or intermediate states
when_to_use: High availability is more important than immediate consistency
Multi-region deployments where synchronous replication is too slow
Social media feeds, recommendations, and analytics that tolerate staleness
Shopping carts, user preferences, and non-critical user state
DNS, CDN, and content distribution systems
Metrics, logging, and observability data pipelines
avoid_when: Financial transactions requiring immediate account balance accuracy
Inventory systems where overselling is unacceptable
Coordination systems where stale reads cause incorrect decisions
User expectations require immediate visibility of their own changes
signals: Replication lag metrics tracked in monitoring dashboards
Conflict resolution functions (last-writer-wins, merge) in codebase
Anti-entropy or read-repair background processes
Application code handling stale reads gracefully
Asynchronous replication configuration between database nodes
Consistency model documented as eventual in system design docs

patterns.name: Circuit Breaker
patterns.description: A stability pattern that prevents an application from repeatedly calling a failing dependency by tripping open after a failure threshold is reached. In the open state, calls fail fast without attempting the downstream call. Periodically allows test requests to check if the dependency has recovered.
principles: Closed state allows all calls; open state rejects all calls immediately
Half-open state allows a limited number of test calls to probe recovery
Failure threshold (count or rate) determines when to trip open
Timeout for the open state determines when to transition to half-open
Separate circuit breakers per dependency prevent correlated failures
Circuit breaker state changes should be logged and alerted on
when_to_use: Calling external services that may become unresponsive or slow
Database connections that fail due to overload or network issues
Preventing cascading failures across microservice dependency chains
API gateway protecting backend services from client-induced overload
Third-party API integrations with unknown reliability characteristics
Any synchronous dependency call that could block or fail repeatedly
avoid_when: Failures are transient and resolved by simple retry
Dependency has no recovery mechanism (circuit will never close)
Local in-process calls that cannot fail independently
Fire-and-forget operations where failure is acceptable
signals: Hystrix, Resilience4j, Polly, or pybreaker in dependencies
Circuit breaker state (OPEN/CLOSED/HALF_OPEN) in health endpoints
Failure count thresholds in service call configuration
Fallback response logic triggered on circuit open
Dashboard showing circuit breaker state across services
Cascading timeout failures in distributed call chains
key_concepts: Failure rate threshold and sliding window

patterns.name: Bulkhead
patterns.description: Isolating components or resources into partitions so that failure in one partition does not cascade to others. Named after ship bulkheads that prevent flooding from sinking the entire vessel. Applied through thread pool isolation, connection pool partitioning, or process separation.
principles: Each critical dependency gets its own isolated resource pool
Failure in one partition is contained and cannot exhaust shared resources
Pool size per partition is tuned based on dependency throughput and latency
Rejected calls due to pool exhaustion should have fallback behavior
Monitoring per-partition utilization detects imbalanced resource allocation
Bulkhead boundaries align with failure domains in the architecture
when_to_use: Multiple downstream dependencies with different reliability profiles
Slow dependency consuming all threads, starving other request types
Multi-tenant systems requiring resource isolation between tenants
Critical vs non-critical request paths needing separate resource pools
Microservice architectures where one dependency failure should not affect others
Database connection pools shared across multiple query types
avoid_when: System has a single dependency and partitioning adds no value
Overall resource pool is too small to partition meaningfully
Dependencies have identical reliability and performance characteristics
Operational complexity of managing multiple pools exceeds benefit
signals: Thread pool per dependency in service configuration
Semaphore-based concurrency limiters per operation type
Kubernetes resource quotas per namespace or pod
Connection pool configuration separated by read/write or by tenant
Hystrix thread pool isolation in circuit breaker configuration
Incident reports showing one slow dependency affecting unrelated requests
key_concepts: Thread pool isolation vs semaphore isolation

patterns.name: Retry with Exponential Backoff
patterns.description: Automatically retrying failed operations with progressively increasing delays between attempts. Gives transient failures time to resolve while preventing retry storms that overwhelm recovering services. Adding jitter prevents synchronized retries from multiple clients.
principles: Only retry on transient errors; permanent errors should fail immediately
Exponential backoff increases wait time between successive retries
Jitter (randomization) prevents thundering herd from synchronized retries
Maximum retry count bounds total retry duration
Retry budget limits the fraction of requests that are retries
Idempotent operations are safe to retry; non-idempotent operations need special handling
when_to_use: Network calls failing due to transient connectivity issues
HTTP 429 (rate limited) or 503 (service unavailable) responses
Database deadlock errors that resolve on retry
Distributed lock acquisition failures
Cloud API calls subject to rate limiting and throttling
Message queue publish failures due to transient broker issues
avoid_when: Error is permanent (authentication failure, invalid input, 4xx)
Operation is not idempotent and retry could cause duplication
Downstream service is known to be down for extended maintenance
Retry latency exceeds acceptable end-to-end response time
Circuit breaker has already opened for the dependency
signals: Retry library (Tenacity, retry, Spring Retry) in dependencies
Exponential backoff configuration with base delay and max retries
Jitter factor in retry delay calculation
Retry count metrics in monitoring dashboards
HTTP client configuration with retry policies
Error classification logic distinguishing retryable vs permanent errors
key_concepts: Retry budget and retry storm prevention
Maximum retry count and total timeout

patterns.name: Timeout Pattern
patterns.description: Setting explicit time bounds on operations to prevent indefinite blocking when a dependency is slow or unresponsive. Converts unbounded waits into bounded failures, enabling the caller to take fallback action. Essential for preventing resource exhaustion from hung connections.
principles: Every network call must have an explicit timeout configured
Timeout values should be based on observed latency percentiles (p99, p999)
Connection timeout and request timeout serve different purposes
Cascading timeouts across a call chain must sum to less than the top-level timeout
Timeout errors should trigger fallback behavior, not just error responses
Default framework timeouts are often too generous for production use
when_to_use: HTTP client calls to external or internal services
Database query execution where slow queries must be killed
Distributed lock acquisition with bounded wait time
File I/O operations over network file systems
Message broker publish and consume operations
Any blocking call where indefinite wait is unacceptable
avoid_when: Operation has a natural completion guarantee and cannot hang
Long-running operations where timeout would waste significant completed work
Streaming operations with chunked transfer that should not be time-bounded
Batch processing where individual item timeout is more appropriate than total
signals: HTTP client timeout configuration (connect_timeout, read_timeout)
Database statement_timeout or query_timeout settings
Socket timeout settings in network client configuration
Timeout exception handling with fallback logic in error handlers
SLA documentation specifying maximum response time per endpoint
Cascading timeout calculation in distributed call chain design docs
key_concepts: Deadline propagation in gRPC and similar frameworks

patterns.name: Fallback Pattern
patterns.description: Providing an alternative response or behavior when the primary operation fails, enabling the system to continue functioning in a degraded mode. Fallback strategies range from cached responses to default values to alternative service calls. Ensures users receive a response even when dependencies are unavailable.
principles: Fallback should provide a meaningful degraded experience, not just an error
Cached previous response is often the most useful fallback
Static default values must be safe and not misleading
Fallback execution must not itself become a failure point
Fallback activation should be monitored and alerted on
Users should be informed when they are receiving degraded data
when_to_use: Service dependency returns errors but stale data is acceptable
Feature toggling allows disabling non-critical features under load
Recommendation engine failure can fall back to popular items
Search service failure can fall back to category browsing
Payment processing can queue for later retry with user notification
Personalization failure can fall back to generic content
avoid_when: Fallback data would be misleading or dangerous (medical, financial)
User explicitly requires real-time accuracy and stale data is harmful
Fallback masks persistent failures that need immediate attention
Fallback implementation is as complex and failure-prone as the primary
signals: Fallback functions in circuit breaker configuration
Try/catch blocks returning cached or default values on error
Feature flags toggling non-critical functionality
Alternative service endpoints configured as backup data sources
Degraded mode indicators in API responses
Stale cache serving logic activated on primary source failure

patterns.name: Graceful Degradation
patterns.description: Systematically reducing system functionality under stress or partial failure to preserve core capabilities. Non-essential features are shed to protect critical operations. Requires pre-defined degradation levels and clear criteria for transitioning between them.
principles: Core business functionality is identified and prioritized for preservation
Degradation levels are pre-defined with clear activation criteria
Non-essential features are shed before essential ones are impacted
Degradation transitions should be automated based on system health signals
Recovery from degraded mode should be automated with hysteresis
Users should be informed of degraded functionality when applicable
when_to_use: System is under extreme load approaching capacity limits
Critical dependency is partially failing or slow
Infrastructure incident affecting non-critical subsystems
Planned maintenance requiring reduced functionality
Cost optimization during off-peak hours by reducing feature set
DDoS mitigation requiring non-essential feature shedding
avoid_when: All features are equally critical and cannot be prioritized
Degradation modes have not been tested and validated
User expectations require full functionality or explicit failure
Regulatory requirements mandate all-or-nothing operation
signals: Feature flags with degradation levels in configuration
Load shedding logic in request processing pipeline
Health check endpoints reporting degraded vs healthy status
Runbook procedures for manually activating degraded mode
SLA documentation defining core vs non-core features
Degradation level monitoring in system dashboards

patterns.name: Chaos Engineering
patterns.description: The discipline of proactively injecting failures into production or staging systems to discover weaknesses before they cause outages. Builds confidence in a system's ability to withstand turbulent conditions. Follows a scientific method: hypothesize, experiment, observe, improve.
principles: Start with a steady-state hypothesis about normal system behavior
Inject realistic failures that could occur in production
Run experiments in production when safe; staging for destructive tests
Minimize blast radius with abort conditions and kill switches
Automate experiments to run continuously, not just one-off
Share findings openly to build organizational resilience knowledge
when_to_use: System has complex dependency chains with unknown failure modes
Moving to microservices or distributed architecture
After significant infrastructure changes or migrations
Validating disaster recovery and failover procedures
Building confidence before high-traffic events (Black Friday, launches)
Training teams on incident response procedures
avoid_when: System lacks basic monitoring and observability
No rollback or abort mechanism exists for experiments
Organization lacks incident response maturity
System is known to have critical unresolved vulnerabilities
Regulatory environment prohibits intentional service disruption
signals: Chaos Monkey, Litmus, or Gremlin in infrastructure tooling
Gameday or chaos experiment schedules in team calendars
Fault injection middleware or sidecars in deployment manifests
Experiment runbooks with hypothesis, procedure, and abort criteria
Post-experiment reports documenting findings and remediation
Kill switch or emergency stop mechanisms for chaos experiments
key_concepts: Blast radius control and abort conditions

patterns.name: Health Check Pattern
patterns.description: Exposing endpoints that report the operational status of a service and its dependencies, enabling automated monitoring, load balancer routing, and orchestrator restart decisions. Distinguishes between liveness (is the process running) and readiness (can it serve traffic). Critical for container orchestration and service mesh operation.
principles: Liveness checks determine if the process should be restarted
Readiness checks determine if the instance should receive traffic
Dependency health should be included in readiness but not liveness
Health checks must be fast and not cause the problems they detect
Startup probes handle slow initialization without premature restart
Health check responses should include structured diagnostic information
when_to_use: Container orchestration (Kubernetes) requires liveness and readiness probes
Load balancer needs to route traffic only to healthy instances
Monitoring systems require structured health status for alerting
Service mesh needs health signals for traffic management decisions
Auto-healing infrastructure requires signals to restart unhealthy instances
Deployment automation needs rollout health gates
avoid_when: Health check implementation is more complex than the service itself
False positive health check failures cause unnecessary restarts
Checking dependency health creates cascading health check failures
Health check endpoint is exposed without authentication on sensitive services
signals: GET /health, /healthz, or /ready endpoints in service routes
Kubernetes livenessProbe and readinessProbe in pod specifications
Health check response JSON with component status breakdown
Load balancer health check configuration targeting specific endpoints
Startup probe configuration for slow-starting applications
Health check failure alerts in monitoring and paging systems
key_concepts: Liveness vs readiness vs startup probes
Health check response format and detail

patterns.name: Dead Letter Queue
patterns.description: A secondary queue that captures messages that cannot be processed successfully after exhausting retry attempts. Prevents poison messages from blocking the main processing queue indefinitely. Enables investigation, manual remediation, and replay of failed messages.
principles: Failed messages are moved to DLQ after max retry count is exhausted
DLQ messages retain original metadata for debugging (timestamp, error, source)
DLQ monitoring and alerting detects processing failures promptly
Replay mechanism enables reprocessing DLQ messages after fixes
DLQ retention policy prevents unbounded growth
Separate DLQs per source queue enable targeted investigation
when_to_use: Message processing can fail due to data issues or transient errors
Poison messages would otherwise block FIFO queue processing
Failed message investigation and remediation is required for compliance
Message replay after bug fix is a common operational procedure
SQS, RabbitMQ, or Kafka consumer groups with processing failures
Event-driven architectures where no message should be silently dropped
avoid_when: Messages can be safely dropped without business impact
Processing failures are always transient and resolved by retry alone
Operational team cannot investigate and remediate DLQ messages
DLQ messages contain sensitive data requiring additional security controls
signals: DLQ configuration in message broker or queue service settings
Max receive count or delivery attempt limit on main queue
DLQ depth monitoring alerts in operations dashboards
Message replay scripts or tools in operations tooling
Error metadata attached to DLQ messages (exception, stack trace)
Periodic DLQ review procedures in team runbooks

patterns.name: Idempotency
patterns.description: Designing operations so that executing them multiple times produces the same result as executing them once. Essential for safe retry behavior in distributed systems where network failures can cause duplicate requests. Typically implemented via idempotency keys stored in a persistent deduplication store.
principles: Same idempotency key with same parameters must produce identical results
Idempotency key storage must be durable and checked atomically with the operation
Key expiration prevents unbounded storage growth
Client-generated idempotency keys (UUID) prevent server-side duplication
Naturally idempotent operations (GET, DELETE by ID) need no additional mechanism
Response caching for idempotent requests returns the original result on replay
when_to_use: Payment processing where duplicate charges are unacceptable
API mutations that may be retried by clients on timeout
Message processing in at-least-once delivery systems
Webhook delivery where the same event may arrive multiple times
Distributed saga steps that may be replayed on coordinator recovery
Database writes from retry-enabled consumers
avoid_when: All operations are naturally idempotent (reads, upserts by natural key)
Exactly-once delivery is guaranteed by the transport layer
Idempotency key storage cost exceeds the cost of occasional duplicates
Operation has no side effects (pure functions)
signals: Idempotency-Key header in API request specifications
Deduplication table or cache storing processed request IDs
Unique constraint on operation identifiers in database schema
Client code generating UUIDs for mutation requests
Message deduplication configuration in queue consumer settings
API documentation specifying idempotent endpoints

patterns.name: Compensating Transaction
patterns.description: Undoing the effects of a previously completed operation by executing a semantically opposite action when a later step in a multi-step process fails. Central to the Saga pattern for distributed transactions. Not a true rollback but a business-level reversal that must be designed for each operation.
principles: Each forward operation must have a defined compensating action
Compensations must be idempotent since they may be retried
Compensations may not perfectly reverse the forward action (e.g., notifications already sent)
Compensation ordering is the reverse of the forward execution order
Compensation state must be persisted to survive coordinator failures
Some operations are inherently non-compensable and require alternative strategies
when_to_use: Saga pattern orchestrating multi-service distributed transactions
Long-running business processes with multiple steps across services
Booking systems where reservations must be released on failure
Order processing where payment must be refunded if fulfillment fails
Workflow engines managing multi-step processes with rollback capability
Event sourcing systems where compensating events reverse prior events
avoid_when: Two-phase commit provides sufficient atomicity for the use case
All operations are within a single database transaction boundary
Compensating actions are not semantically meaningful for the domain
Compensation logic complexity exceeds the benefit of the saga approach
signals: Saga orchestrator or choreography framework in codebase
Compensate or undo methods paired with forward action methods
Compensation log or journal persisting undo steps
Refund, cancel, or release operations in service API contracts
Workflow state machine with forward and backward transitions
Event store with compensating event types

patterns.name: Load Balancer
patterns.description: Distributing incoming requests across multiple backend instances to optimize resource utilization, maximize throughput, and ensure no single instance is overwhelmed. Operates at Layer 4 (TCP) or Layer 7 (HTTP) with algorithm choices affecting distribution fairness and session handling.
principles: Load balancing algorithm must match the workload characteristics
Health checks remove unhealthy backends from the rotation
Session affinity trades optimal distribution for session consistency
Connection draining ensures in-flight requests complete during removal
Layer 7 load balancers can route based on content (path, headers)
Multiple load balancer tiers prevent single points of failure
when_to_use: Multiple application instances serving the same traffic
Horizontal scaling requires traffic distribution across nodes
Blue-green or canary deployments need traffic shifting
SSL termination should be offloaded from application servers
Geographic routing to nearest datacenter or region
WebSocket or gRPC requiring connection-level load balancing
avoid_when: Single instance deployment with no scaling requirement
Client-side load balancing is more appropriate (service mesh)
Peer-to-peer architecture with no central ingress point
Load balancer introduces unacceptable single-hop latency
signals: NGINX, HAProxy, ALB, or Envoy in infrastructure configuration
Upstream or backend server pool definitions in load balancer config
Health check endpoint configuration in load balancer settings
Sticky session or affinity configuration for stateful services
SSL certificate configuration on load balancer for TLS termination
Traffic weight or percentage configuration for canary deployments

patterns.name: Rate Limiting
patterns.description: Controlling the rate at which clients can make requests to protect services from overload, abuse, and resource exhaustion. Enforced at API gateways, application middleware, or infrastructure level. Returns HTTP 429 when limits are exceeded, with Retry-After headers guiding client behavior.
principles: Rate limits should be set based on capacity planning and SLA commitments
Per-client limits prevent a single client from monopolizing resources
Global limits protect the service from aggregate overload
Rate limit responses must include retry guidance (Retry-After header)
