Different rate limits for different API endpoints or tiers
Rate limit state must be shared across distributed service instances
when_to_use: Public API exposed to untrusted or unpredictable clients
Preventing abuse and denial-of-service attacks
Enforcing fair usage across multi-tenant API consumers
Protecting downstream services from upstream traffic spikes
Monetizing API access with tiered usage plans
Compliance with third-party API rate limits when acting as a proxy
avoid_when: Internal service-to-service calls within a trust boundary
Backpressure mechanisms provide sufficient flow control
Traffic volume is consistently below service capacity
Rate limiting would violate SLA guarantees to premium clients
signals: HTTP 429 responses in API logs and client error handling
Rate limit headers (X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining) in responses
API gateway rate limiting configuration (Kong, Apigee, AWS API Gateway)
Redis-based rate limit counter storage for distributed enforcement
Tiered API key or subscription plan with different rate limits
Rate limit exceeded alerts in monitoring dashboards
key_concepts: Fixed window vs sliding window rate limiting
Token bucket and leaky bucket algorithms
Rate limit response headers and retry guidance

patterns.name: Backpressure
patterns.description: A flow control mechanism where a slower consumer signals upstream producers to reduce their sending rate, preventing buffer overflow and resource exhaustion. Propagates demand signals backward through the processing pipeline. Essential for reactive and streaming system designs.
principles: Consumers must signal their processing capacity to producers
Unbounded buffers mask backpressure and eventually cause failures
Backpressure must propagate through all stages of a pipeline
Dropping messages is a valid backpressure strategy when appropriate
Reactive Streams specification standardizes backpressure semantics
TCP flow control is the network-level analog of application backpressure
when_to_use: Stream processing pipelines with variable stage throughput
Producer is faster than consumer and buffering is bounded
Reactive application frameworks (RxJava, Reactor, Akka Streams)
Log or event ingestion systems receiving bursty traffic
Inter-service communication where consumer speed varies
Database write paths receiving faster input than write throughput
avoid_when: Producers cannot meaningfully slow down (external event sources)
Buffering capacity is effectively unlimited and cost-acceptable
Message loss is unacceptable and blocking producers is preferable
System throughput is consistently balanced between stages
signals: Reactive Streams (Publisher/Subscriber/Subscription) in codebase
Buffer overflow errors or warnings in application logs
Queue depth growing unbounded in monitoring dashboards
Flow control configuration in stream processing frameworks
Consumer request(n) calls limiting demand in reactive pipelines
TCP window size changes indicating network-level backpressure

patterns.name: Throttling
patterns.description: Deliberately limiting the rate of operation execution to protect system resources or comply with external constraints. Unlike rate limiting which rejects excess requests, throttling may queue, delay, or degrade excess requests. Provides smoother traffic shaping than hard rejection.
principles: Throttling smooths traffic patterns rather than rejecting excess
Multiple throttling dimensions (per-user, per-tenant, global) provide layered protection
Throttle limits should be configurable without deployment
Throttled requests should receive clear feedback about wait time
Priority-based throttling preserves capacity for critical operations
Throttle metrics must be monitored to detect sustained overload
when_to_use: Multi-tenant systems requiring fair resource allocation
Complying with downstream service rate limits
Smoothing bursty traffic patterns before reaching backend services
Protecting shared resources (database, cache) from thundering herd
Cost control by limiting expensive operation throughput
Preventing noisy neighbor effects in shared infrastructure
avoid_when: Hard rejection is acceptable and simpler to implement
Queuing throttled requests would consume excessive memory
Real-time requirements cannot tolerate added delay from queuing
Downstream systems handle their own rate limiting
signals: Request queuing with delayed processing in middleware
Throttle percentage or rate parameters in service configuration
Tenant-level throughput limits in multi-tenant platform settings
Delayed response metrics indicating throttled request handling
Priority tagging on requests for selective throttling
Cost-based throttling on expensive API operations
key_concepts: Queuing vs rejection vs degradation responses
Throttle feedback headers and retry guidance

patterns.name: Priority Queue
patterns.description: Processing work items in order of priority rather than arrival time, ensuring critical operations are handled before less important ones. Enables service differentiation under constrained capacity. Requires clear priority classification and starvation prevention for lower priorities.
principles: Priority levels must be well-defined and consistently assigned
Starvation prevention ensures low-priority items eventually process
Priority escalation (aging) promotes long-waiting items
Separate queues per priority level simplify implementation
Priority inversion must be detected and mitigated
Monitoring per-priority queue depth and latency reveals imbalances
when_to_use: Mixed workloads with different urgency levels sharing processing capacity
Customer tier differentiation (premium vs free tier) for processing order
Incident response systems where severity determines handling order
Real-time vs batch processing sharing the same pipeline
Order processing where express shipping takes priority
Alert routing where critical alerts are processed before informational ones
avoid_when: All items have equal priority and FIFO ordering is sufficient
Priority assignment logic is complex and error-prone
Starvation of low-priority items is unacceptable
Processing capacity always exceeds demand (no queuing needed)
signals: Multiple queue channels or topics with priority naming
Priority field or header in message payload schema
Weighted consumer allocation across priority queues
SLA documentation with priority-differentiated response times
Customer tier mapping to processing priority levels
Queue depth monitoring split by priority level

patterns.name: Fair Scheduling
patterns.description: Allocating processing resources proportionally across multiple tenants, users, or workload classes to prevent any single consumer from monopolizing shared capacity. Ensures equitable access while allowing unused capacity to be redistributed. Common in multi-tenant platforms and shared compute clusters.
principles: Each consumer receives a guaranteed minimum share of resources
Unused capacity is redistributed to active consumers
Weight-based allocation enables differentiated service tiers
Preemption may be necessary to enforce fair share guarantees
Fair share calculation must update dynamically as demand changes
Monitoring per-consumer resource usage detects fairness violations
when_to_use: Multi-tenant platforms with shared compute or storage resources
Cluster scheduling (YARN, Kubernetes) for mixed workloads
API platforms with multiple consumer teams sharing backend capacity
Database connection allocation across multiple application services
Shared CI/CD pipeline capacity across development teams
Network bandwidth allocation across competing traffic flows
avoid_when: Single tenant or single consumer exclusively owns the resource
Strict priority ordering is required over fair sharing
Resource pool is large enough that contention never occurs
Fairness computation overhead exceeds the benefit for small pools
signals: YARN Fair Scheduler or Kubernetes ResourceQuota in cluster config
Per-tenant resource quota configuration in platform settings
Weight or share assignments per consumer in scheduling config
Resource usage dashboards broken down by tenant or team
Preemption events in scheduler logs
Fair share violation alerts in monitoring systems

patterns.name: Token Bucket
patterns.description: A rate limiting algorithm that allows bursts of traffic up to a bucket capacity while enforcing a long-term average rate. Tokens are added at a fixed rate and consumed per request; requests are rejected or queued when the bucket is empty. Provides a balance between strict rate enforcement and burst tolerance.
principles: Tokens are added at a constant rate up to the bucket capacity
Each request consumes one or more tokens from the bucket
Burst size is limited by the bucket capacity (maximum tokens)
Sustained rate is limited by the token refill rate
Empty bucket causes request rejection or queuing
Multiple bucket dimensions (per-user, per-endpoint) enable layered limiting
when_to_use: API rate limiting that should allow short traffic bursts
Network traffic shaping with burst tolerance
Metering resource consumption with burst credits
SaaS API quota enforcement with burst allowance
Smoothing traffic while permitting natural request clustering
Client-side rate limiting to avoid exceeding upstream limits
avoid_when: Strictly uniform rate is required (use leaky bucket instead)
Burst traffic would overwhelm downstream systems
Rate limiting granularity is too fine for token bucket overhead
Simpler fixed-window counting is sufficient
signals: Token bucket or bucket capacity parameters in rate limiter config
Burst size and refill rate configuration in API gateway
Rate limiter allowing occasional request spikes above average rate
Token balance tracking in rate limiter state (Redis, memory)
AWS API Gateway throttle and burst limit settings
Custom rate limiter implementation with bucket abstraction
key_concepts: Token bucket vs leaky bucket comparison

patterns.name: Leaky Bucket
patterns.description: A rate limiting algorithm that processes requests at a constant output rate regardless of input burstiness. Incoming requests fill a bucket; the bucket drains at a fixed rate. If the bucket overflows, excess requests are rejected. Produces smooth, predictable output traffic.
principles: Output rate is constant regardless of input rate variations
Bucket depth determines maximum burst absorption capacity
Overflow causes request rejection or dropping
FIFO queuing in the bucket preserves request ordering
Fixed drain rate provides predictable downstream load
Simpler to implement than token bucket but less burst-friendly
when_to_use: Downstream systems require perfectly smooth traffic flow
Network traffic shaping for quality of service enforcement
Processing pipeline requires constant throughput rate
Protecting databases from bursty write traffic
Achieving predictable resource utilization
Rate limiting where burst tolerance is undesirable
avoid_when: Burst traffic should be allowed when capacity permits
FIFO ordering constraint causes head-of-line blocking issues
Queue memory for burst absorption is too expensive
Token bucket's burst tolerance better matches the traffic pattern
signals: Constant output rate despite variable input rate in traffic graphs
Queue depth configuration for burst absorption
Traffic shaping policy with guaranteed constant rate
FIFO request queue draining at fixed rate in middleware
Network QoS configuration using leaky bucket model
Outbound rate control on API client libraries
key_concepts: Bucket depth sizing for burst absorption
Comparison with token bucket for burst handling

patterns.name: Adaptive Concurrency Control
patterns.description: Dynamically adjusting the allowed concurrency level based on observed system behavior, typically latency and error rates. Uses algorithms like gradient-based or Vegas-style TCP congestion control adapted for application-level concurrency. Automatically finds the optimal concurrency without manual tuning.
principles: Concurrency limit adjusts automatically based on latency and error signals
No manual tuning of concurrency limits required
Gradient descent algorithms converge on optimal concurrency
Latency increase indicates approaching system capacity
Concurrency reduction on error prevents cascading failure
Minimum and maximum concurrency bounds prevent extreme values
when_to_use: Service-to-service calls where optimal concurrency is unknown
Variable backend capacity (autoscaling) makes static limits ineffective
Proxy or gateway fronting backends with unknown capacity
Systems where manual concurrency tuning is impractical
Microservice architectures with diverse and changing backends
Envoy, gRPC, or service mesh requiring automatic concurrency limits
avoid_when: Backend capacity is well-known and stable for static limits
Latency variation is inherent to the workload and not load-related
Algorithm convergence time is too slow for rapidly changing capacity
Simple fixed concurrency limits provide sufficient protection
signals: Netflix concurrency-limits library or similar in dependencies
Envoy adaptive concurrency filter in proxy configuration
Dynamic concurrency limit value in runtime metrics
Gradient-based or AIMD algorithm configuration parameters
Concurrency limit oscillation visible in time-series monitoring
Latency-based load shedding tied to concurrency limit
key_concepts: Integration with circuit breaker and load balancer

patterns.name: Connection Draining
patterns.description: Gracefully removing a backend instance from a load balancer by allowing in-flight requests to complete while refusing new connections. Prevents request failures during deployments, scaling events, or maintenance. Essential for zero-downtime operations in production environments.
principles: New connections are directed away from the draining instance immediately
In-flight requests are allowed to complete within a timeout window
Drain timeout must be set based on maximum expected request duration
Long-lived connections (WebSocket, gRPC streams) require special handling
Health check failure should trigger draining, not abrupt removal
Draining state should be observable in monitoring for deployment tracking
when_to_use: Rolling deployments requiring zero-downtime instance replacement
Autoscaling scale-in events removing instances from the pool
Planned maintenance window for individual backend instances
Container orchestration pod termination with graceful shutdown
Load balancer backend deregistration for any operational reason
Blue-green deployment traffic cutover
avoid_when: Requests are stateless and idempotent, making abrupt removal safe
Drain timeout would delay deployment unacceptably
All connections are short-lived and complete in milliseconds
Emergency shutdown requires immediate termination
signals: Deregistration delay configuration on load balancer target groups
Kubernetes terminationGracePeriodSeconds in pod spec
PreStop lifecycle hook in container orchestration
Connection drain timeout parameter in load balancer settings
SIGTERM handler in application code completing in-flight requests
Deployment strategy configuration specifying drain behavior

patterns.name: Traffic Shaping
patterns.description: Controlling the volume and rate of network traffic to optimize resource usage, enforce policies, and ensure quality of service. Encompasses techniques like bandwidth throttling, request prioritization, and traffic policing. Applied at network, infrastructure, and application layers.
principles: Traffic classification determines which shaping policy applies
Shaping delays traffic to conform to a desired rate profile
Policing drops or marks traffic exceeding the rate limit
Quality of service (QoS) classes receive differentiated treatment
Traffic shaping must be coordinated across all ingress points
Monitoring shaped vs unshaped traffic reveals policy effectiveness
when_to_use: WAN links require bandwidth allocation across traffic classes
API traffic needs different treatment by priority or customer tier
Canary deployments require percentage-based traffic splitting
Service mesh traffic management for A/B testing and rollouts
Protecting shared infrastructure from noisy neighbor effects
Compliance with network usage policies or bandwidth contracts
avoid_when: Network bandwidth vastly exceeds demand
Traffic classification is not feasible or meaningful
Shaping latency is unacceptable for the application
Simpler rate limiting provides sufficient control
signals: Istio, Envoy, or Linkerd traffic management rules in configuration
Canary or weighted routing configuration in service mesh
Network QoS marking (DSCP) in infrastructure configuration
Traffic percentage split between deployment versions
Bandwidth limit configuration on network interfaces or policies
Traffic class definitions with differentiated handling rules
